nodes.name: 科托河
nodes.summary: 科托河是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 裏海盆地
nodes.summary: 裏海盆地是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 不列顛群島
nodes.summary: 不列顛群島是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联4种鸟类，包括小天鹅、红头潜鸭、斑背潜鸭等4种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 湿地
nodes.summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

nodes.name: 卡纳塔克邦
nodes.summary: 卡纳塔克邦是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括南亞鴇、黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 克爾曼省
nodes.summary: 克爾曼省是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 海岸湿地
nodes.summary: 海湾、泻湖、盐沼与河口组成的复合湿地。

nodes.name: 泰國北部地區
nodes.summary: 泰國北部地區是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 黑鹳
nodes.summary: 黑鹳（Black stork，学名 Ciconia nigra），分类上属于鹳形目，并归入鹳科，在图谱中标记为无危（LC）物种，已关联25个分布地点节点，常见栖息地包括开阔山地、海岸湿地、河口等5项，主要威胁包括猎捕压力。

nodes.name: 阿尔泰山脉
nodes.summary: 阿尔泰山脉是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括岩雷鸟、黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 巴伐利亚
nodes.summary: 巴伐利亚是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括松鸡、黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 勃艮第
nodes.summary: 勃艮第是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 南高加索
nodes.summary: 南高加索是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 埃斯特雷马杜拉
nodes.summary: 埃斯特雷马杜拉是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 河口
nodes.summary: 淡水与海水交汇形成的高生产力边缘生态系统。

nodes.name: 湿地
nodes.summary: 湿地是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联30种鸟类，包括鵲鵝、澳洲硬尾鸭、黑天鹅等30种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 沼泽
nodes.summary: 具有较高含水量和密生植被的浅水环境。

nodes.name: 西伯利亚
nodes.summary: 西伯利亚是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联22种鸟类，包括红胸黑雁、雪雁、凍原豆雁等22种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 南部非洲
nodes.summary: 南部非洲是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联6种鸟类，包括灰头麻鸭、Swainson's spurfowl、Rüppell's korhaan等6种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 卡伦河 (伊朗)
nodes.summary: 卡伦河 (伊朗)是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 胡齊斯坦省
nodes.summary: 胡齊斯坦省是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 开阔山地
nodes.summary: 地形开阔且便于大翼展鸟类活动的山地区域。

nodes.name: 東亞塞拜然省
nodes.summary: 東亞塞拜然省是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 华南地区
nodes.summary: 华南地区是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 猎捕压力
nodes.summary: 直接猎捕或误捕造成额外死亡。

nodes.name: 新疆维吾尔自治区
nodes.summary: 新疆维吾尔自治区是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 鹳科
nodes.summary: 鹳科（Ciconiidae）是图谱中的科级分类节点，当前关联17种鸟类，如钳嘴鹳、非洲秃鹳、Greater adjutant等17种鸟类。

nodes.name: 旁遮普邦
nodes.summary: 旁遮普邦是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 阿登 (地区)
nodes.summary: 阿登 (地区)是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: LC
nodes.summary: LC表示 IUCN 无危等级，当前关联1443种鸟类，如鸵鸟、鸸鹋、南方鶴鴕等1443种鸟类。

nodes.name: 印度东北部
nodes.summary: 印度东北部是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联7种鸟类，包括白颊山鹧鸪、灰孔雀雉、红腿斑秧鸡等7种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 礼萨呼罗珊省
nodes.summary: 礼萨呼罗珊省是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 印度北部
nodes.summary: 印度北部是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括黑鹳、长尾夜鹰，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 鹳形目
nodes.summary: 鹳形目（Ciconiiformes）是图谱中的目级分类节点，当前关联17种鸟类，如钳嘴鹳、非洲秃鹳、Greater adjutant等17种鸟类。

nodes.name: 法萊梅河
nodes.summary: 法萊梅河是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括黑鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: thumb|Black stork in flight During the summer, the black stork is found from Eastern Asia (Siberia and northern China) west to Central Europe, reaching Estonia in the north, Poland, Lower Saxony and Bavaria in Germany, the Czech Republic, Hungary, Italy and Greece in the south, with an outlying population in the central-southwest region of the Iberian Peninsula (Extremadura and surrounding provinces of Spain, plus Portugal). It is migratory, wintering in tropical Africa and Asia, although certain populations of black storks are sedentary or dispersive. An isolated population exists in Southern Africa, where the species is more numerous in the east, in eastern South Africa and Mozambique, and is also found in Zimbabwe, Eswatini, Botswana and less commonly Namibia. Most of the black storks that summer in Europe migrate to Africa, with those from western Germany and points west heading south via the Iberian Peninsula and the rest via Turkey and the Levant

It is migratory, wintering in tropical Africa and Asia, although certain populations of black storks are sedentary or dispersive. links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Those flying via Spain spend winter in the Falémé River basin of eastern Senegal, Guinea, southern Mauritania, Ivory Coast, Sierra Leone and western and central Mali, while those flying via the Sinai end up in northern Ethiopia, the Kotto River basin in the Central African Republic, the Mbokou river basin in Chad and northeastern Nigeria. Black storks summering in West Asia migrate to northern and northeastern India, ranging mainly from Punjab south to Karnataka, and Africa. They are occasional visitors to Sri Lanka. Those summering further east in eastern Russia and China winter mainly in southern China, and occasionally in Hong Kong, Myanmar, northern Thailand, and Laos. They were first recorded in western Myanmar in 1998.{{cite journal|last1=Robson|first1=C

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: F.|year=1998|title=A birdwatching visit to the Chin Hills, West Burma (Myanmar), with notes from nearby areas|url=http://orientalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/Robson-Chin.pdf|journal=Forktail|volume=13|pages=109–120|access-date=9 July 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304202821/http://orientalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/Robson-Chin.pdf|archive-date=4 March 2016|url-status=live}} The black stork prefers more wooded areas than the better-known white stork, and breeds in large marshy wetlands with interspersed coniferous or broadleaved woodlands, but also inhabits hills and mountains with sufficient networks of creeks. It usually inhabits ponds, rivers, edges of lakes, estuaries and other freshwater wetlands. The black stork does inhabit more agricultural areas in the Caspian lowlands, but even here it avoids close contact with people. Its wintering habitat in India comprises reservoirs or rivers with nearby scrub or forest, which provide trees that black storks can roost in at night. In southern Africa it is found in shallow water in rivers or lakes, or swamps, but is occasionally encountered on dry land. After disappearing from Belgium before the onset of the 20th century, it has returned to breed in the Belgian Ardennes, Luxembourg and Burgundy, France, by 2000. It appears to be increasing in numbers in Spain and Portugal, where the population was estimated at 405 to 483 pairs in 2006. The black stork is a rare vagrant to the British Isles, turning up in the warmer months—particularly in spring—generally in the south and east

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sf7UBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA90 | page=90 | publisher= Bloomsbury Publishing |location=London| year= 2010 |orig-date=1986 | isbn=978-1-4081-3835-9}} It is not abundant in the western parts of its distribution, but more densely inhabits eastern Transcaucasia. Further east, it has been recorded from locations across Iran, though little is known about its habits there; breeding has been recorded from near Aliabad in Fars province, Khabr National Park in Kerman province, Karun river in Khuzestan province, Qaranqu River in East Azarbaijan province, and Aliabad river in Razavi Khorasan province. The population has declined in Iran due to draining of wetlands.{{cite book | title=The Complete Fauna of Iran | first= Eskandar |last=Firouz| publisher= I.B

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sf7UBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA90 | page=90 | publisher= Bloomsbury Publishing |location=London| year= 2010 |orig-date=1986 | isbn=978-1-4081-3835-9}} It is not abundant in the western parts of its distribution, but more densely inhabits eastern Transcaucasia. Further east, it has been recorded from locations across Iran, though little is known about its habits there; breeding has been recorded from near Aliabad in Fars province, Khabr National Park in Kerman province, Karun river in Khuzestan province, Qaranqu River in East Azarbaijan province, and Aliabad river in Razavi Khorasan province. The population has declined in Iran due to draining of wetlands.{{cite book | title=The Complete Fauna of Iran | first= Eskandar |last=Firouz| publisher= I.B

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: South of Siberia, it breeds in Xinjiang, northwestern China, northern Mongolia south to the Altai Mountains, and northeastern China south to the vicinity of Beijing

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: South of Siberia, it breeds in Xinjiang, northwestern China, northern Mongolia south to the Altai Mountains, and northeastern China south to the vicinity of Beijing

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: Breeding pairs usually build nests in large forest trees—most commonly deciduous but also coniferous—which can be seen from long distances, as well as on large boulders, or under overhanging ledges in mountainous areas

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: F.|year=1998|title=A birdwatching visit to the Chin Hills, West Burma (Myanmar), with notes from nearby areas|url=http://orientalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/Robson-Chin.pdf|journal=Forktail|volume=13|pages=109–120|access-date=9 July 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304202821/http://orientalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/Robson-Chin.pdf|archive-date=4 March 2016|url-status=live}} The black stork prefers more wooded areas than the better-known white stork, and breeds in large marshy wetlands with interspersed coniferous or broadleaved woodlands, but also inhabits hills and mountains with sufficient networks of creeks. It usually inhabits ponds, rivers, edges of lakes, estuaries and other freshwater wetlands. The black stork does inhabit more agricultural areas in the Caspian lowlands, but even here it avoids close contact with people. Its wintering habitat in India comprises reservoirs or rivers with nearby scrub or forest, which provide trees that black storks can roost in at night. In southern Africa it is found in shallow water in rivers or lakes, or swamps, but is occasionally encountered on dry land. After disappearing from Belgium before the onset of the 20th century, it has returned to breed in the Belgian Ardennes, Luxembourg and Burgundy, France, by 2000. It appears to be increasing in numbers in Spain and Portugal, where the population was estimated at 405 to 483 pairs in 2006. The black stork is a rare vagrant to the British Isles, turning up in the warmer months—particularly in spring—generally in the south and east

links.evidence: status = LC

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: | year=2014 | title=Truede og sjældne ynglefugle i Danmark 1998-2012 | url=https://pub.dof.dk/artikler/650/download/doft-108-2014-1-144-truede-og-sjaeldne-ynglefugle-i-danmark-1998-2012 | journal=Dansk Ornitologisk Forenings Tidsskrift | volume=108 | issue=1 }} The population in India—a major wintering ground—is declining. Previously a regular winter visitor to the Mai Po Marshes, it is now seldom seen there, and appears to be in decline in China overall. Its habitat is changing rapidly in much of eastern Europe and Asia. Various conservation measures have been taken, including Wetlands International's Conservation Action Plan for African black storks, which focuses on improving the wintering conditions of the birds which breed in Europe. It is protected by the Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds (AEWA) and the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES). Hunters threaten the black stork in some countries of southern Europe and Asia, such as Pakistan, and breeding populations may have been eliminated there. The black stork vanished from the Ticino River valley in northern Italy, with hunting a likely contributor
